8:45 AM – Morning Lab Meeting – fMRI Data Analysis and Protocol Refinement  
In the neuroimaging lab, they lead a 45-minute team huddle with postdocs, grad students, and a clinical psychologist. They review raw fMRI data from 12 new participants with combat-related PTSD, focusing on hyperactivity in the amygdala and hypoactivation in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex during fear extinction trials. They challenge the team to refine their motion-correction algorithms to reduce false positives in insula activation—a known confound in trauma populations with heightened startle responses. They also introduce a new protocol: simultaneous heart rate and galvanic skin response recording during scans to capture real-time autonomic correlates.

activity:
8:45 AM – Morning Lab Meeting – fMRI Data Analysis and Protocol Refinement  
In the neuroimaging lab, they lead a 45-minute team huddle with postdocs, grad students, and a clinical psychologist. They review raw fMRI data from 12 new participants with combat-related PTSD, focusing on hyperactivity in the amygdala and hypoactivation in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex during fear extinction trials. They challenge the team to refine their motion-correction algorithms to reduce false positives in insula activation—a known confound in trauma populations with heightened startle responses. They also introduce a new protocol: simultaneous heart rate and galvanic skin response recording during scans to capture real-time autonomic correlates.
prompt: If the autonomic data we’re collecting reveals that some participants have decoupled heart rate from skin conductance during fear extinction, does that mean their nervous systems have developed a new survival strategy—one our models don’t yet recognize?

10:30 AM – Clinical Consultation with a PTSD Patient (via Telehealth)  
They conduct a 50-minute telehealth session with a 34-year-old refugee who developed PTSD after surviving a bombing. Though not their primary clinical role, they maintain a small caseload to stay grounded in patient narratives. During the session, they listen carefully to somatic reports—“My chest feels like it’s collapsing when I hear sirens”—and correlate these with prior fMRI findings showing exaggerated insular reactivity to auditory trauma cues. They document these phenomenological details to inform future stimulus design in their lab’s next fMRI paradigm.
prompt: How do I design an fMRI auditory stimulus that captures the emotional weight of a siren for this patient without retraumatizing them, when the same sound might evoke numbness in another PTSD survivor or even nostalgia in someone who grew up near emergency services?
